comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight has arrived, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 267,446th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This was my fifty-fifth published book, one of 2019's few breaks from the relentless pace of my tentacle fiction publications, and a very bittersweet experience for me. A long time ago, back in the 1980s and 1990s, I had a dream that failed. I dreamed of building a bridge between the modern Hermeticism of the Golden Dawn tradition and the older and richer tradition of Renaissance Hermeticism; I hoped to attract the interest of Golden Dawn students to such things as sacred geometry, elemental medicine, the Art of Memory, and the Hermetically inspired swordsmanship of Gerard Thibault, all of which were standard among Renaissance Hermeticists and could become part of a renewed Hermetic spirituality. I tried my best to achieve that, using the limited means and skills I had at the time, and got zero response. Later in the 1990s, recognizing the failure of the dream, I put more of my efforts into Druidry, and that ended up succeeding beyond my expectations -- so I shelved the older project.

This volume contains the essays and articles I wrote to try to build the bridge I'd imagined. Predictably, despite the efforts of the publisher, the republication got no more attention in the Hermetic scene than my original efforts had. I still hope that someday somebody will build that bridge and enrich modern Hermeticism with the extraordinary wealth of the older tradition, but it's pretty clear at this stage that the person in question won't be me. If the book interests you, you can get a copy here if you're in the US and here elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. (Seriously, folks, I have to delete up to a dozen attempted post-midnight comments every Tuesday morning. That's a waste of your time and mine alike.)  See you next week!***

comments.title: Draft of "Hail Holy Forebears" and Question on Practical SOP Intention Overlap
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To all who marked it, I hope you had a happy Thanksgiving full of the food and folks that bring you the most cheer, and may the coming end of the year bring closings and fulfillments that you seek.

To Share: I've posted a draft of the last prayer for the Heathen Rosary undertaking, with the hope of getting some thoughts on how to make it better from anyone willing to share: https://jpowellrussell.com/#heathen_rosary_draft_hail_holy_forebears

To Ask: Earlier this year, I set a practical intention for self-transformation with the SOP and performed it with that intention weekly on a planetary day that corresponded with the goal until divination suggested it I should stop (before starting, divination had said "do this until you get a divination that says it's time"). Since then, I've been saying an affirmation for the same goal, and will keep doing so until a year-and-a-day after I started the process (so far, so good).

My question is, would it be best to hold off on other self-transformation intentions for the SOP to support unity of will while still working with the prior affirmation? Or is this something that varies from situation to situation, and so is a matter for meditation and divination?

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Draft of "Hail Holy Forebears" and Question on Practical SOP Intention Overlap
replies.content:
I added an affirmation midway through the year, as events picked up speed in my life, and let go of another one. This was after several years of keeping the same one at least for a year, sometimes two. I think the discipline of keeping the ones I did first for a year or more helped in strengthening the will. But then intuition, just as important, told me to go with something else to better align with destiny. I've been sticking with that one, and a new one I started at my usual year mark, for several months now. I think the key here is keeping open to those points when intuition is telling us to align will with destiny. Pay attention to those and they might be a good point of change, just like your divination is guiding you.

Citrine Hibernating Eagle Flaneur

comments.title: Blessing new home and appurtenances
comments.content:
So my family and I recently ended our annus horribilis of subsisting in motels and have settled into a small but pleasant apartment in a beautiful town.

We’ve been here for nearly a month and it seems high time we did some sort of blessing for our home. The only problem is that I don’t happen to know any.

I’m basically a Hellenic Golden Dawnie but my family members are Episcopalians, so this might complicate things a bit. I’m open to any constructive suggestions for something fairly simple and easy, if that can be managed.

Also, I’ve just acquired a drawing table and related appurtenances that are to be the instruments of some important-to-me creative and professional projects. I fancy some blessings for those might be good too: sort of like launching a ship, only without smashing a champagne bottle on it.

replies.title: Re: Blessing new home and appurtenances
replies.content:
I'm not up to date on every Christian tradition, but you might ask whether your family's Episcopalian church offers blessed chalk for "chalking the door" during Epiphany (conveniently celebrated first week of January so not too long to wait). A priest will bless sticks of chalk at the church and offer them for the congregation to take home. The chalk is used to mark "20 ✝ C ✝ M ✝ B ✝ 25" above the front and back door with the C, M, and B standing for the initials of the three Magi of the Epiphany and the 20 and 25 being the first and last 2 digits of the current year.

If your local church does not do chalk blessing, I don't see why you couldn't still get an ordinary stick of school or sidewalk chalk of any color and chalking your door with an ad-hoc prayer on Epiphany as a private practice.

replies.title: Re: Blessing new home and appurtenances
replies.content: I'd encourage you to pour a libation to the gods you worship, and especially to Hestia, the goddess of the hearth fire, asking her to bless your home. You don't have to let your family know about it if they'd be upset.

replies.title: Re: Amulet refresh
replies.content: The bag is just a container, so can be reused as many times as desired. It's not a problem if the nail's tarnished; it needs to be changed if it's visibly corroded or rusty. When you dispose of it, if possible, bury it in the ground.

comments.title: Magical Resistance still in denial
comments.content:
John Beckett put out a post titled "Where Are The Gods in All This," going on about the war his side thinks is going on in the Otherworld.

Someone commented asking if he and his audience are sure the gods are even on their side to begin with. The reply was there is no evidence to think the gods are not in perfect lockstep with every goal of the left.

Wonder what it will take for them to realize the gods have their own agenda and Wokeism isn't it?

replies.title: Re: Magical Resistance still in denial
replies.content:
They will go to their graves convinced that they're the Good People and the gods are on their side. As I discussed in a blog post quite a few years ago:

"The beings who try to stop the Changer and keep the world the same just keep doing whatever they were doing when the Changer arrives: the man with the board keeps carving tree trunks, the man with the many-pointed weapons keeps looking around — and there they are today, the beaver beside his dam, the deer on the hill. Having refused change, they become unable to change, and keep on going through the motions of their failed plans forever. That’s exactly what Trump’s opponents have been doing since his candidacy hit its stride, and more particularly since his inauguration. 'From now on your name is Protester,' says the Changer, and sticks a pussy hat on the person’s head and a placard in her hands. 'When the People come, they'll meme you for the keks.'"

replies.title: Re: Magical Resistance still in denial
replies.content: My thoughts on the matter: "Same place they were during the Age of Aries when they were more generally worshipped, a time famously known for its utopian cultures, egalitarianism, tolerance (where does the word 'barbarian' come from again?), and concern for the dignity of all people (how many cultures back then didn't practice slavery?)."

replies.title: Re: Magical Resistance still in denial
replies.content:
Anon,

That article has several claims about the gods and other things that had my jaw drop because it sounded so much like a fundamentalist Christian in pagan drag and were unbelievable…even for Beckett. I’ve been reading him for years to keep a gauge on the goings of the neopagan end of the Druid scene.

From a magical perspective, I find it profoundly interesting that he recently started identifying as a witch instead of or along with being a Druid, and how that identification has changed how he writes, or rather ratcheted up his rhetoric. Since he did that he has locked into the protestant puritan worldview of the witch/christian and projected it onto everything, going so far as to talk about the war in heaven, and ironically this otherworld war of the gods is just another version of that. It’s really spooky but informative to watch.

Best,
Dean

comments.title: Juliet Ashley
comments.content:
Hello, I have been wondering about the origins of Juliet Ashley's Sphere of Protection concept. I read your Trilithon article, where you make a persuasive case for a Jungian provenance for its *form*, but I am thinking rather of its *purpose*.

It occurs to me that since Ashley was involved with Edgar Cayce, she may have been influenced by the Prayer of Protection used at the beginning of "A Search For God" small meditation group meetings, which goes like this: "As we approach the Throne of Grace, Beauty, and Might, we seek the protection that is found in the thought of the Christ." (No accompanying gestures, prayer-direction, etc. are given.) To your knowledge, is there a better candidate within the ceremonial-magic tradition (e.g. the Golden Dawn LBRP) for the idea that spiritual practice is potentially dangerous and in need of protection?

Incidentally, I understand that a group within the AODA is studying Ashley. I wrote, hoping to make contact, but never heard back. (I know little about the magical tradition, but am familiar with the Cayce material.) Thank you very much.

https://aoda.org/history-heritage-of-the-aoda/dr-juliet-ashley/

replies.title: Re: Juliet Ashley
replies.content:
Interesting! I'm not very familiar with Cayce's work, so this is worth knowing.

As for ceremonial magic, yes -- it's a central concept all through the tradition that there are potentially hostile or dangerous spiritual energies in the cosmos and that protection is necessary. The LBRP is to my mind the most likely archetype for the SoP, though it got to Ashley at at least one remove -- AE Waite, from whom Ashley got her Golden Dawn charter, replaced the pentagram ritual with a comparable ritual using the cross. My guess is that this is what inspired Ashley to put in the elemental symbols instead.

replies.title: Re: Juliet Ashley
replies.content:
Thank you!

--Dawei

replies.title: Re: Juliet Ashley
replies.content:
I think just about any magico-spiritual tradition that I'm aware of has ideas about, and practices to satisfy the need for, protection from hostile, dangerous, hostile, or just discordant, energies. Thee are differences in style and vehemence, and on levels of anxiety (or paranoia!), but the concern is there.

I have come across a Rosicrucian adaptation of such a ritual (not the Rose Cross Ritual). but I'd casually assumed it was a recent development. Perhaps it could trace its way back to Waite as well.

Smaragdopolitanus

comments.title: Spirituality in Partnership
comments.content:
Hi John. Reading your Paths of Wisdom book at the moment. Thanks for your great work on it.

Question about my husband’s experience in meditation: when we first met a handful of years ago, we meditated nightly together. He was deeply interested in having a mystical experience in meditation like the many I’ve had over the years. With the exception of 1-2 “inner impressions” that felt important right at the beginning, he didn’t experience anything that would confirm for him an intelligent inner world or “higher realm” for lack of better language. Even after meditating daily for a year. He doesn’t really meditate anymore because i think he became discouraged and maybe doesn’t know if it’s possible for him.

Do you have any tips for someone who desires to experience a novel state of consciousness in meditation but who hasn’t gotten there yet even with great effort? He’s really struggling to stay open to the possibility of the “other side” bc he can’t feel it even when he’s reached for it.

I know we each have our own paths to walk and some are less “magical” than others but I wonder if you have any tips for him or someone who wants to feel the currents of magic/spirit as a total beginner?

An example of what I’m looking for are exercises that could get someone familiar on a basic level with the spiritual energy underwriting this reality. An example I saw you give once was moving energy through one’s hands as a way to confirm the existence of chi. (Sorry if I’m misremembering that somehow.) we did try that but he didn’t feel anything in his hands which perplexed me as someone who feels lots and lots of energy in my hands when I move it with my consciousness.

Any tips?

Blessings of peace to you this year. I see that it’s been full of big change.

replies.title: Re: Spirituality in Partnership
replies.content:
The problem is that this sort of approach is a classic invitation to failure. If your meditation is motivated by a craving for phenomena, you won't get any results at all. To get anywhere with meditation, you have to set aside all those expectations, desires, and cravings, and just attend to the process. This isn't easy.

More generally, it's been my constant experience that people who approach spirituality that way -- with an attitude of "show me something to prove to me that it's real" -- have a divided will on the subject. They want results but they also fear them, and the closer they get to anything the stronger the fear becomes. I've seen people of this kind experience something strange and then immediately go to work rationalizing it out of existence, or even panicking and insisting that they never experienced anything at all.

The inner ambivalence has to be resolved before spiritual experience becomes possible. The spiritual world is all around us all the time. It's not that it's not there -- it's that most of us are hiding from it.

replies.title: Re: Spirituality in Partnership
replies.content:
This hits close to home.

I had the reverse experience: a series of quite unsettling spiritual experiences years ago without (so far as I know) inviting them, that convinced me there actually is something out there, and then a quest for spiritual knowledge and practices that could shield me from the darker aspects of what I had experienced.

I've spent a few years now on daily magical practices (SOP, divination, discursive meditation) and it has been a long time since I've experienced anything I would call spooky or mystical, though helpful synchronicities increased after I took up daily practice.

I would hazard to say that my will is divided between wanting to experience those phenomena again (curiosity) and wanting to block them out completely (fear). I don't let that division bother me though, as my interest in the work is now in becoming the kind of person who can lead a healthy, useful life regardless of whether or not I'm able to see spirits or what have you. If I'm meant to resolve that internal division and perceive other worlds again in this lifetime, it will happen in the natural course of healing and correcting my character and habits through daily work.

Hope this perspective helps.

replies.title: Re: Spirituality in Partnership
replies.content: Thank you for this. That's a much less common pattern, but it's good to know that it's something people are experiencing.

replies.title: Re: Spirituality in Partnership
replies.content:
Re: A much less common pattern...

Right, which is why it took me so long to get a clue as to why things happened that way for me.

After doing my daily practices for a couple of years, I had a dream that pretty clearly was about previous lifetimes (first time I had ever had any clue about previous incarnations). It was unlike any other dream I've had, in that it was unusually vivid and unusually realistic- nothing random or fantastical in it. Long story short, I seem to have been involved with occult group work gone awry at some point in the distant past, and am making up for the mistake in this life.

replies.title: Re: Couple questions
replies.content:
In the Western Middle Ages there were two distinct, yet comfortably coexisting, ways of aligning the elements and with the four quarters.

The celestial alignment placed fire and air opposite one another at the equinoxes (which align with the East and West), earth and water opposite one another at the solstices (which align with South and North). This governs the spheres from the moon upwards to the stars.

The terrestrial alignment, in contrast, put fire and water opposite one another at the south and the north, earth and air opposite one another at the west and east. This governs things under the lunar sphere, i.e., the earth.

I don't happen to know of any single medieval text that spells these two alignments out together, but the terrestrial one shows up in Medieval alchemy and the celestial one in Medieval astrology. Which isn't to say that there may actually be texts that treat them together, just that I haven't happened upon such a text so far.

replies.title: Re: Couple questions
replies.content:
That's very interesting, thank you. It seems like there isn't any absolute correlation between correspondences, more just a consistency within schools thing. It's probably a personal issue that I keep looking for absolutes where none exist.

Your comment jogged my memory of another correspondence that I was meaning to look into and completely forgot about. I was reading a book on Mayan mythology and one of their creation myths had the cardinal directions corresponding to black, white, red, and yellow, and I was meaning to try and figure out if there was a bridge with the alchemical associations to those colors. Now I'll have to go look up what direction was what and think on it.

replies.title: Re: Couple questions
replies.content:
Actually, it's not open to free choice, or a difference between schools. Each set of correspondences is absolute within its own realm. You're right to look for absolutes, but they are a trifle more complex than you have supposed.

Rather, the correspondences differ according to just where in the universe you are (or are thinking about). In the Middle Ages, if you were dealing with the sublunary spheres, you used the terrestrial alignment exclusively, never the celestial one; within that realm, the terrestrial correspondences are absolute

If, however, you were dealing with the spheres from the moon and upward through all the planets and the stars until you get to the sphere of the angels, you used the celestial alignment exclusively, never the terrestrial one; within that realm the celestial correspondences are absolute.

The very highest spheres, of each on the nine orders of angels and beyond, have no room for any such alignments.

replies.title: Re: Buddhism
replies.content: You can't de-condition people who don't want to be de-conditioned. comments.title: Marriage questions

Some things that come to mind:
1) strong venus, jupiter in the sky?
2) sextile/trines transits to personal planets?
3) strong 4th, 7th house aspects?
4) Lord of 7th or 4th activated by transits?

replies.title: Re: Marriage questions
replies.content:
1) Elections are more flexible than that. You want to have the lords of the 1st and 7th well dignified, since those rule the spouses; if they want children, the lord of the 5th should also be well dignified; then it's a matter of seeing what else you can get -- a waxing Moon is good, a favorable aspect between Sun and Moon or between Mars and Venus even better, good aspects with the benefics are always good, and so on. The important thing is to get as many favorable and as few unfavorable indications into the chart as you can, knowing that there are no perfect elections.

2) In American tradition, the point at which the two principals say "I do" makes the marriage. Before then, either or both can back out; afterwards, it takes a divorce. That's the moment to elect for, in other words. Everything else is preparation or follow-through.

0) Start by forgetting every single thing about magic you've learned from D&D. It's all wrong, full stop, end of sentence. You could do worse, but only by relying on the Harry Potter novels. Seriously, D&D has about as much to do with real magic as Young Frankenstein has to do with real science.

1) Elementals don't have forms in that sense. They are embodied in their elements, but there are no boundaries separating one of them from another. If you want to see what an earth elemental looks like, go look at some dirt. They're in there. Clairvoyants tend to see vaguely humanoid forms when encountering spirits, but that's a function of the same process that turns energies into humanlike forms in our dreams. Geoffrey Hodson's useful book The Kingdom of the Gods has illustrations that are closer to my own experiences. Here's the guardian spirit of a region of the landscape:

[IMAGE:0]

2) Every energy is an entity, and vice versa. It's a little like asking whether light consists of waves or particles.

3 & 4) Your last suggestion is very close to the traditional lore. A hot spring will have a spirit, and an assortment of undines and salamanders will be dancing in there as well. In the same way, a mountain has a spirit -- the Greeks used to call it an oread -- but there will be various more or less subordinate spirits active in various parts of the mountain, and plenty of elementals in and around it. The unseen world is a very busy place.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For what it's worth, it seems pretty likely Gygax was at least somewhat familiar with Theosophical ideas and some other earlier 20th century occult concepts, but it also seems like he didn't treat those any differently than the pulp fiction of Jack Vance, Poul Anderson, and so forth - in other words, as sources to be strip-mined for interesting and gameable ideas (perfect example: the Astral Plane as a place full of Dead Gods and Forgotten Ideas, where anyone who's visiting in his astral body has a (tactically significant!) silver cord floating off into the distance). Likewise with the elementals - Gygax was an aficionado of medieval bestiaries, and it seems likely he treated Paracelsus the same way - sylphs, undines, salamanders, and gnomes were all around as "elemental kin" by at least AD&D 2nd edition (I can't track down any discussion of whether they were introduced earlier, but they were definitely around by the 2E Monstrous Manual, where I first encountered them), but they were just another elementally-flavored creature to get some variety in there.

Oh, and on Bonewits, I haven't read Authentic Thaumaturgy, though it caught my interest when I first started getting into magic, having been an RPG player most of my life and with his Real Magic the first book on the topic I read, but after getting into our host's stuff,
I've come to share his assessment of Bonewits's stuff. That said, the bibliography in Real Magic is pretty decent, from what little I know, especially if you want options from a wide variety of traditions (that book's whole deal was trying to synthesize across traditions, rather than going deep on one. These days I have my doubts on the value of the synthesis presented, but it's a nice, broad list of books).

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In case you know much computational science, I get the impression that, if you consider a hierarchical multiresolution simulation scheme ( https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multigrid_method#Algorithm ), an elemental is in one way analogous to the data structure that would be specific to each particular point in the hierarchy of locations and feature-sizes. (This analogy would work better if it was the kind of adaptive algorithm that had reason to keep learned calculation-accelerating "hints" at each of its multigrid nodes, but there's not usually call for doing things that way.)

But maybe an elemental is also analogous to the working group in a game worldbuilding team that was tasked with working out the details and history of a particular region (earth elementals) or movable personage or party (fluid elementals), and keeping track of constraints of fictional continuity and consistency involving that region or party.

But maybe an elemental is also analogous to a bureau or microbureau, or regional division or desk, in a bureaucracy or corporation (or industry sector) tasked with managing, tracking, or providing a service across multiple regions and subregions.

But maybe an elemental is also analogous to a trainee, or a position for a succession of trainees. In this case the division between elemental types might be only as rigid as divisions between professions, or between artistic or scientific specializations.

In such cases, yeah, there'd not be a lot of reason to expect an elemental to have a body whose layout in the relevant space resembles anything familiar from zooanatomy. I don't know why Paracelsus gives physical measurements for gnomes. Maybe he was grouping things together differently and assimilating miners' reports about "Bergmännlein" with earth elementals of this more "rationalized", natural-philosophy-conceptually-distilled sort. If it helps you feel better, you can imagine depictions of elementals as being the "heraldic" version, like how the stylized convention for depicting heraldic lions is quite different from ordinary lions. (Especially heraldic salamanders, that's already a thing.)

One day a couple of years ago I repeated a simple three-word affirmation - one I would rather keep private - for several minutes very emphatically and with great feeling.

The next day out of the blue a complete stranger walked up to me at the mall, pointed at me and repeated the same exact affirmation, but in second person rather than first person. He did this repeatedly and with great emphasis.

Not once in a thousand years would this happen by mere coincidence. This person somehow picked up on the thought I had been generating and repeated it back to me. So now I know for a fact, through direct personal experience, that telepathy is real and can confidently tell you so.

replies.title: Re: Mind Reading
replies.content: While the individuals involved don't reflect the neurological baseline of most individuals, non-verbal autists who have learned to communicate via spelling have been reporting their own telepathy ever since they could point to letters to "speak".  The actual geography of their ability is astonishing, as real world geography doesn't seem to be an impediment to its function.  A non-verbal autistic person in Michigan is apparently fully capable of carrying on a mental conversation with someone in Norway on a regular basis.  There are also numerous reports among them that there is a mental "place" they can go to called "The Hill" to talk to each other, kind of like a telepathic chat room, which is also sometimes visited by the dead.  So then there is also evidence that telepathy can bleed over into mediumship, if that's the right word.

comments.title: the universe as origami
comments.content:
JMG,

I've been musing on a metaphor that seems friendlier to me to explain creation and the forces and beings that inhabit it than emanation from a central stillness. I'm wondering if you know of any tradition that has articulated this better than I'm about to attempt.

There is only one being in the universe, as has been said many times here and elsewhere, and everything is this being experiencing itself. In order to create a duality/polarity/opposite to experience, it folds itself, creating two faces that stand opposite each other, each face experiencing the other as opposite, as a separate consciousness. Each side of the fold can continue to fold again and refold countless times, creating something that resembles a complex brain structure (caution: this is a metaphor so limited) or a very complicated origami. Folds can also be unfolded. For example, incarnation is a fold into form, which then can be unfolded and refolded again.

I have this unfortunate image in the metaphor based on emanation of the poor little divine sparks being sent out into the darkness of the seventh level to begin the journey back, and yes, I know that the seven levels are present always, but it seems so lost and lonely, everything wandering around in the dark trying to figure out where the hell they are and what they're supposed to do and not having yet developed the wherewithal to do so.

If I look at everything as just a fold in the one being, I am aware of everything as a face of the divine, which is present at all times: the virus that is giving me a cold, my irritating sister, the winter cold coming down, my comfortable chair, the fire in my woodstove, healing forces and life principles, myself... each just one of the folds in the divine. And like a thumb feeling the finger that touches it, the finger feeling the thumb that touches it, and the person feeling both, the divine is feeling both me and the cold virus, for example, as we experience each other.

I keep musing on the universe as origami, but surely I'm not the only one who thought of this. Would love to read a better articulated version of it if you know of one.

By the way, I had never heard of Fire Cider till you posted it, made a batch, and man, I am sooooo thankful I did. Worked wonders on my sore throat. I'm pretty sure it nipped an ear infection in the bud. I have been glugging some into my green tea.

Thank you!
-Myriam

replies.title: Re: the universe as origami
replies.content:
If that metaphor works for you, by all means use it. It's certainly not an unreasonable one.

As for fire cider, glad to hear it.

comments.title: Subtle bodies gender change

comments.title: Paedrig's Pentacles
comments.content:
So, I have been noticing an increasing sense of dark miasma floating about. So many are in a state of shock and despair right now, amd I think their personal auras of doom are starting to pollute the aether a bit.

The intense nature of some breakdowns I've seen are starting to resemble possesion or at least the influence of malevolent spirits, and some people I care about are getting... weird. Am I correct in stating the people in states of shock or despair sort of open themselves up to being fed on be entities that then try to promote the dark feelings and thought patterns that enable them to feed on our auras? I've been curious, for quite some time now, about the process of driving away miasma, dark energy, and unpleasant/unwanted entities. Its starting to seem more and more important right about now, though.

I already practice the sphere of protection, though I may be losing the space in which I practice it - it's outside anyway. While it was wonderful to do the ritual bemeath the stars and planets, in a stone circle aligned to the cardinal directions by the north star - it's also much, much too cold for all that.

So, until I can recreate my circle, I'm wondering, aside from the southern conjure bath - which is also too cold for the weather now - and protection amulet - which I cant keep around my neck and now hangs around the stick shift in my rickety old truck, hopefully holding it together - what else is available for pushing away the mass of negative energy swirling around the world right now? What if I encounter someone suffering from a nonphysical parasite, feeding upon their despair? Anything I can put in my toolkit aside from sage sticks?

Any and all advice are always appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content:
Well, what kinds of magic do you know how to do? Different kinds of magic have different tools for dealing with miasmatic forces. A hoodoo doctor will use one set of methods, a Golden Dawn initiate a very different set, and both may get good results but with wholly different skills.

Two things, though. First, this kind of work is not for beginners. It requires skill, knowledge, and power built through years of practice.

Second, you can't free someone of miasma if they don't want to be freed from it. That's especially an issue now. There are certainly people on the left who are motivated by love, but there are rather too many who are motivated by hatred, rage, and a jealous clinging to class privilege in the face of upstart populism -- and these latter can't be freed of negative energies until they let go of the emotional states that hold those energies in place. Lacking that willingness, things may get very ugly for them.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content:
My magical knowledge is certainly at the level of an apprentice - I know a bit, but I certainly need guidance. Aside from the rituals mentioned, I created the talisman to Jupiter in a ceremony largely guided by intuition, amd I have some familiarity with the LBRP and very, very basic Golden Dawn work, as well as the lessons I have picked up from your Doctrine and Ritual book club.

I have been quite interested in driving away dark spirits in large part because of a pervasive sense that I have something I need to get rid of - various amateurish attempts were made, and I think I pushed the being away for a time, but... It seems to feed on my third chakra, and indeed my confidence is almost constantly undermined by unemding shame. I can trace it back to various sources - my mother, my expectations of life and myself - but it feels like a hole that has a parasite in it, that only avoids becoming a complete abyss due to an excess of energy flowing there, almost to compensate.

The thing is, I have recently had incredible success overcoming this thought/emotion pattern and I literally feel myself becomign cleaner, more invigorated - my fortunes are literally turning around. Yet I feel as though there is some final step, some key I need to complete, and I feel like there must be a way to share this blessing - maybe its because I'm trying to embody a Jovian mentality, but I want to share this feeling of being finally free of a dark weight.

I fully understand that you cannot force someone to give up a bad spirit or a bad habit - you can lead a horse to water, but you cant make it drink. But I want some basic tools, to assist myself and others who are willing to try and drive away the darkness.

So, two questions - what self cleansing ritual do you suggest to be the capstone of an almost year long period of purification, using regular sphere of protection rituals, discursive meditation, and divination. Seconsly, is there anything you would recommend for cleansing others? If you dont think I'm ready to help others, I'll put aside that thought. But I feel driven to complete this process within myself at long last.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content: I have no answer for you because you're asking for something far more specific than the tradition provides. Rituals that specific are crafted by each practitioner for himself or herself -- and this presupposes a level of mastery that, as an apprentice, you probably haven't reached. "A self cleansing ritual...as the capstone of an almost year long period of purification" isn't a basic tool...

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content:
Ah. So what I'm gathering is - I seem to be wading in over my head. And you dont want to hand me any tools I might use to hurt myself.

Which is totally fair, and probably what I need to hear. Thanks for this. I'll meditate on it.
Maybe what I really need to do is confront the very idea I need to be cleansed - maybe I'm creating the very problem I'm trying to solve here.

As always, your guidance is appreciated - even when, and especially when, its not what I want to hear. Those are always the most important lessons, after all.

replies.title: Re: Paedrig's Pentacles
replies.content: Or I might be saying what I'm actually saying, which is that you have to make your own tools and you don't have the skills yet. It's a little frustrating when something I try to make clear gets reinterpreted out of existence...

comments.title: What Is Enlightenment?
comments.content:
A few weeks ago, you briefly mentioned "the enlightened" in one your MM answers: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/303892.html?thread=52212756#cmt52212756

I am wondering what enlightenment is and how one achieves it in the teachings of the Western Mystery Tradition? I find the concept of enlightenment interesting but the idea itself seems to vary even within closely related religious traditions (e.g. Theravada and Mahayana don't seem to fully agree on what Nibbana/Nirvana is, to cite just one example).

replies.title: Re: What Is Enlightenment?
replies.content: In the Golden Section Fellowship we describe enlightenment as that state in which wisdom and revelation are permanent conditions of the soul. Wisdom can be described as an intuitive inner sense of truth. Revelation can be described as a flash of insight from outside the self that illumines previously unknown truth. Yes, all this differs from other definitions!

comments.title: Tarot Oddity
comments.content:
Hey, JMG, I know this is pretty much down to the wire and it is not a question per se, but I'd just like to share what (at least to me) is a bit of a tarot oddity.

I have been doing daily readings, off and on, for the past few years. Over the course of 384 daily readings I had every card in the tarot deck show up many, many times. Except for one: the Seven of Cups. I would even occasionally check my deck to make sure that the card was there. Yup - every time I checked, it was there!

Well, today, for the first time ever (daily reading #385) the elusive Seven of Cups appeared as the central (Situation) card in my three-card spread. So - was there anything particularly special about today? In a way, yes: I ended up having to drive my car on seven separate errands. Fortunately none of the errands involved severed heads, or snakes, or a glowing person under a white sheet! :). Needless to say, if this is the typical portent of the Seven of Cups, I don't mind almost never getting it as I wasted close to 3 hours today driving hither and thither and I am tuckered out.

I hope you had a great day!

Ron M

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Are the magical properties of herbs that haven't yet been used in a working affected by banishing done in the same room, assuming they're not within the area where the ritual is done?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Let me make sure we're interpreting your words the same way. There's a room in which a banishing is performed. The herbs are not in the room when the banishing is performed, but they are brought in at some later time, and they do not remain in the room when other banishings are performed.

If that's what you have in mind, no, the properties of the herbs are unaffected. It's as though you washed and dried the jar in which you meant to keep the herbs, before putting them inside. The herbs don't get retroactively washed!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The herbs are in the room while the banishing is performed, but not within the circle defined by the ritual.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Then they will lose potency. The effect of the banishings extends outside the circle to include the space within the room.
